data.title: Investors Expect U.K. Spending Cuts at Spring Statement — Market Talk
data.description: Investors expect the U.K. to announce spending cuts at the upcoming Spring Statement on March 26, Quilter Investors' latest investor trends survey shows. Almost half of the respondents see spending cuts "as the most optimal way forward," Quilter says. A third of the respondents expect cuts would be combined with additional tax rises, the survey says. The BOE is likely to cut interest rates by more than markets expect as rates appear too restrictive, the survey shows. The Spring Statement is one of two statements each year by the U.K. treasury chief on the economy. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: India's Trent falls as Kotak cuts PT, citing revenue pressure
data.description:
** Shares of retail stores operator Trent fall 3.6% to 4,819.2 rupees

** Stock among top pct losers on Nifty 50 index , which is down 0.43%

** Kotak Institutional Securities cuts PT to 5,150 rupees from 5,550 rupees, maintains "reduce" rating

** Continue to see pressure on co's revenue on account of its densely-located newer Westside and Zudio stores, brokerage says

** Lowers its revenue estimates for Westside for FY2026-27, which it says will result in ~0.3%-1.0% rev cut for Trent

** Rev reduction affects margins, leading to a 1%-5% cut in EPS - Kotak

** Avg rating of 18 analysts is a 'buy;' their median PT is 6,123.5 rupees - LSEG

** TREN has shed 30% so far this year vs a 5% drop in Nifty 50

XPeng ADR Target Price Raised to $29.00 From $13.70 by Citigroup>XPEV
FTSE Straits Times Index Closes 0.4% Lower at 3899.07
New Frontier Minerals Sells Broken Hill East Project to Impact Minerals
data.description:
New Frontier Minerals agreed to sell its BHA No. 1 subsidiary, which holds the Broken Hill East project in Australia, to Impact Minerals in an all-share deal.

Under the agreement, Impact Minerals will issue new shares totaling $275,000 to the mining company and assume responsibility for a 2% net smelter return royalty payable for tenements held by the nickel-copper-PGE project, according to a Monday filing.

The transaction is expected to close later on the same day.

data.title: German Industrial Uptick Increases Chance Economy Will Expand in 1Q — Market Talk
data.description: The rise in German industrial production in January gives hope that output won't fall in the first quarter, Commerzbank's Ralph Solveen says in a note. The 2.0% jump in production was above the average for the fourth quarter of last year, and more than makes up for the 1.5% decline in December. That rise also increases the chance that the German economy as a whole will grow slightly again in 1Q, after contracting 0.2% in the final quarter of 2024, Solveen says. However, while January's increase in industry can be attributed to a significant rise in Germany's key auto sector, car production remains a weak point of German industry, he cautions, with signs of hope originating more from other sectors.(edward.frankl@wsj.com)

data.title: Hays Short-Term Pain Could Lead to Long-Term Gain — Market Talk
data.description: Hays's profitability is likely going to remain under pressure in the short run amid macroeconomic headwinds, RBC Capital Markets analyst Karl Green says in a research note. The U.K. recruitment company is likely to halve its ordinary dividend for the full year 2025 to preserve cash and ensure its financial stability, he says. By strengthening its balance sheet now, Hays will be in a better position to return more cash to shareholders when the business conditions improve, Green notes. The investment bank keeps its outperform rating on the stock as it sees material share price upside potential in the medium term. Hays shares are down 0.7% at 76.25 pence. (maitane.sardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Hong Kong Stocks Slip Below 24,000 Points on Deflation Concerns amid Trade Tensions; SMIC Drops 5%, Wise Ally International Soars 109%
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks fell below 24,000 points on Monday as consumer prices again showed contraction in February, albeit lower than polled by analysts, raising fears of deflation among the investors on top of the growing trade and geopolitical tensions.

The Hang Seng Index fell 1.85%, or 447.81 points, to close at 23,783.49. The Hang Seng China Enterprises Index dropped 2.12%, or 189.02 points, to end at 8,725.01.

China's consumer price index contracted 0.7% year over year in February, the first time the indicator returned to negative territory since January 2024. Analysts polled by Reuters estimated CPI to have contracted 0.5%.

The producer price index continued contracting, this time by 2.2% in February, easing from a 2.3% slide in January and lower than a 2.1% decline forecast by analysts polled by the news agency. The indicator has been falling since September 2022.

On the geopolitical front, China slapped extra tariffs against selected Canadian imports such as rapeseed oil, oil cakes and peas, aquatic products, and pork, effective March 20.

The US Trade Representative will also conduct a probe on China-manufactured "legacy" chip models used for consumer electronics. The hearing could mean more tariffs against China. Semiconductor Manufacturing International, or SMIC , fell nearly 5% on Monday.

In corporate news, Chifeng Jilong Gold Mining , witnessed a tepid stock market debut as its shares opened flat on their maiden trading in Hong Kong Monday morning. It closed unchanged at HK$13.72.

Wise Ally International's shares soared nearly 109% as the company expects an attributable profit of between HK$60 million and HK$80 million for the year 2024, up from HK$1.2 million a year prior.

Tongguan Gold Group unit Grand Gallant Investments agreed to purchase Wise Trend Investment from Jumbo China for up to HK$400 million, subject to shareholder approval. Shares of the company jumped nearly 27% on Monday's close.

data.title: India's Sterlite Technologies drops as CFO resigns

** Shares of Sterlite Technologies fall 3.8% to 82.5 rupees

** Stock has shed 25% this year, on top of a ~20% decline in 2024

data.title: Gold Futures Flat as Market Awaits U.S. Economic Data — Market Talk
data.description:
0919 GMT - Gold futures are broadly flat in relatively muted trading. Futures are flat on $2,912.10 a troy ounce. The precious metal sits up 0.4% on week on lingering safe-haven demand and a weaker U.S. dollar. That said, gold is trading in a tight range as markets appear to be pricing out the inflationary effect of president Trump's tariff policies given constant reversals, Sucden Financial's Daria Efanova says in a note. Market attention is fixed on Wednesday's U.S. Consumer Price Index data, and Producer Price Index data set for release on Thursday. The economic data could provide more clues as to the Federal Reserve's pathway to monetary policy easing. Lower interest rates typically raise the appeal of non-interest bearing bullion. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0914 GMT - 2025 could be a key year for humanoid robots launches, Daiwa analysts say in a research note. Some Chinese robot makers, like Unitree and AgiBot, have already reduced unit costs to around 100,000 yuan, with the potential for further reductions of more than 50% upon mass-production, they say. Daiwa expects more than one million humanoid robot units to be produced by 2030, while further AI breakthroughs could enhance robots' responsiveness and accelerate their adoption. They anticipate that humanoid robots will be initially used in manufacturing and logistics before expanding into the construction and mining industries. Several leading global tech companies, including Huawei and Meta, have already announced humanoid robot products. Daiwa expects these development to boost sentiment, establish an entirely new supply chain, and particularly benefit component suppliers. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0904 GMT - Bitcoin falls as cryptocurrencies come under pressure from U.S. regulatory uncertainty and widespread risk aversion, Zaye Capital Markets analyst Naeem Aslam says in a note. A crypto summit at the White House on Friday did not provide any details on President Trump's plans for regulation of the sector. Markets are also grappling with concerns over global trade tensions, deflationary pressures in China and the prospect of a weakening U.S. economy, Aslam says. Bitcoin falls 5.5% to $81,493 after hitting a one-week low of $80,085 overnight, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0840 GMT - The euro consolidates after last week's rally against the dollar as markets await news on German fiscal policy and Ukraine peace talks, ING analyst Chris Turner says in a note. A key area of focus is on whether German Chancellor-in-waiting Friedrich Merz can get his 500 billion-euro infrastructure fund through parliament, he says. He is in discussions with the Greens political party and the Bundestag lower house is expected to vote on the measures on March 18. "Headlines on whether the Greens are playing ball this week could trigger some volatility in the euro." The euro falls 0.2% to $1.0820 after hitting a four-month high of $1.0890 Friday, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0840 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds fall as investors expect more interest rate reductions from the Bank of England due to dwindling spending and growth. "Weak business and consumer sentiment may result in further rate cuts from the Bank of England," Lindsay James, investment strategist at Quilter Investors says in a note. The 10-year gilt yield falls 2 basis points to 4.637%, Tradeweb data shows. The 2-year gilt yield declines 1bp to 4.193%. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0815 GMT - India's consumer inflation is expected to have eased to 3.8% on year in February, according to the median estimate of 11 economists polled by The Wall Street Journal. That would compare with the 4.3% rise in January's CPI. Economists mostly expect broad-based declines in food and vegetable prices to drag inflation lower. Inflation is unlikely to rebound, giving the central bank room to ease rates to support the economy, Capital Economics' Shilan Shah and Joe Maher write in a note. "Weak domestic demand, higher crop output and muted manufacturing costs are likely to offset upside risks from a weak currency," Nomura research analysts say in a note. Both expect an additional 75 bps of rate cuts this year, which would bring the repo rate to 5.5% by the year's end. The inflation data are due Wednesday. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0814 GMT - There may be some hurdles for the Bank of Thailand to deliver a consecutive rate cut in April, Citi Research economists say in a note. The central bank had already factored in some of the risks associated with additional U.S. tariff hikes when delivering its surprise rate cut in February, they write. Citi lowers its 2025 and 2026 inflation forecasts to 0.9% and 1.0%, respectively, from 1.0% and 1.4%. Its 2025 inflation forecast is below the BOT's, reinforcing the risk of inflation undershooting the central bank's target for a second consecutive year. The pressure for another rate cut will persist, particularly as falling credit growth alleviates concerns about the financial stability costs of monetary easing, they say. Citi reiterates its expectation for another 25 bps cut in August.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0812 GMT - Eurozone 10-year government-bond yield spreads over German Bunds mostly narrow but should remain choppy amid European Union plans to ease debt rules, says Commerzbank Research's Rainer Guntermann in a note. "Eurozone government-bond spreads should also remain choppy as there could be more color about the EU's plan to relax debt rules from tonight's Eurogroup meeting," the rates strategist says. Germany is pushing for a permanent change of the EU's Stability and Growth Pact to exclude defense spending to avoid new joint debt, Guntermann says. The 10-year French OAT-German Bund yield spread falls 1.5 basis points to just below 72bps, while the 10-year Italian BTP-German Bund yield spread is flat at 111.5bps, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0806 GMT - The Canadian dollar could rise in the medium term if Canada's newly announced prime minister Mark Carney successfully counters U.S. tariffs, Swissquote Bank analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya says in a note. However, in the short-run the currency faces selling pressure as the Bank of Canada is expected to cut interest rates by another 25 basis points on Wednesday, she says. Carney was named prime minister after winning the Liberal Party leadership. The former governor of Bank of Canada and Bank of England succeeds Justin Trudeau, who announced in January that he would step down after his approval rating plummeted. Carney said he would keep retaliatory tariffs until "Americans show us respect." USD/CAD falls 0.1% to 1.4361. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0803 GMT - Germany's proposed increase in spending is likely to limit interest-rate cuts by the European Central Bank, ING analysts say in a note. The ECB is likely to keep interest rates on hold in April and make one cut before the summer, ING says. The planned additional spending is likely to boost economic growth, but it would take time for the positive effects to be seen, the analysts say. "Capital investment takes time to deploy; shovel-ready projects aren't always readily available." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0751 GMT - From a fair value perspective, ING rates strategists see strong reasons for the 10-year German Bund yield to rise towards 3%, with recent headlines and data points generally supporting higher yields, they say in a note. However, the tides could shift quickly, causing long-end government bond yields to fall faster than short-end bond yields--a phenomenon called "bull flattening," they say. "Any setbacks, be it in terms of actual spending, geopolitical tensions, or growth headwinds could easily bull flatten the curve again," the strategists say. The 10-year Bund yield last trades 2.2 bps higher at 2.853%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - The European Central Bank might be too pessimistic about the eurozone's growth chances this year as a planned spending drive looks set to boost activity, Pantheon Macroeconomics' Claus Vistesen and Melanie Debono write in a note to clients. The central bank last week cuts its estimates for output growth in the currency area, pointing notably to the risk of U.S. import tariffs. But those tariffs might not materialise in a sustained fashion, Vistesen and Debono says. And new plans in Germany and across the wider EU to spend more on infrastructure and defense offer a countervailing shot in the arm, they say. "[L]ast week's fiscal announcements point to more balanced risks further out," they say. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: New Delhi Television Says Reliance Infrastructure Withdraws INR100 Billion Defamation Suit
data.description:
New Delhi Television said the defamation suit filed by Reliance Infrastructure and others, seeking 100 billion Indian rupees in damages over the company's coverage of the Rafale deal, has been unconditionally withdrawn.

The City Civil Court at Ahmedabad disposed of the case on March 8, according to an Indian bourse filing on Saturday.

data.title: European Truck Shares Fall Following Traton Guidance — Market Talk
data.description: Shares of European truck makers trade lower in early European trading after Traton says it expects the global economy will lose momentum this year. In Germany, shares of Daimler Truck are 3.2% down while in Italy, Iveco shares are around 2.1% lower. In Sweden, Volvo shares are down 2%. Traton says that after selling 1% fewer vehicles and achieving flat incoming orders last year, it expects demand for trucks to decline in its core markets this year. Traton shares trade 6.3% lower to 35.80 euros. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

data.title: Eneos Plans $3 Billion IPO of Metals Subsidiary — Update
data.description:
By Kosaku Narioka

Eneos Holdings plans to spin off its metals subsidiary through an initial public offering that is expected to raise nearly $3 billion, the biggest listing in Japan in more than six years.

The Tokyo-based energy giant said Monday that it plans to offer a majority of shares in the wholly owned subsidiary, JX Advanced Metals, in the offering that would raise as much as 438.6 billion yen, equivalent to $2.96 billion. The listing is scheduled for March 19.

Eneos said in February that it would use part of the proceeds to fund investments needed to expand the supply of next-generation energy sources, such as synthetic fuels and hydrogen. No new shares will be issued.

The company has said that JX Advanced Metals, which makes materials used in the semiconductor and information-technology industries, will be able to accelerate capital investments as well as research and development as a listed company.

The Tokyo Stock Exchange hosted several large offerings in recent months after the benchmark Nikkei Stock Average hit record highs last year.

In October, the IPO by subway operator Tokyo Metro raised about $2.31 billion, making it the largest since SoftBank Corp.'s $21 billion IPO in 2018, according to data from Dealogic.

Eneos said it will sell up to 534.9 million shares of JX Advanced Metals, or 57.6% of the total outstanding, at Y820 each, equivalent to $5.54. JX Advanced Metals said earlier this month that it expected to offer shares at around Y815 apiece.

Also on Monday, Japan Post Holdings said it will offer $4 billion of its shares in its banking unit. The postal and financial services giant said a total of about 410 million shares of Japan Post Bank will be offered at Y1,444 each, valuing the offering at about Y592 billion, equivalent to $4 billion.

Write to Kosaku Narioka at kosaku.narioka@wsj.com

data.title: Ford to inject up to $4.76 billion into German business, FT reports
data.description:
Ford Motor will inject up to EUR 4.4 billion ($4.76 billion) into its struggling German unit as it tries to revive its car business in Europe, the Financial Times reported on Monday.

($1 = 0.9249 euros)

data.title: Savannah Energy Closes Purchase of SIPEC in Nigeria
data.description:
Savannah Energy finalized its purchase of Sinopec International Petroleum Exploration and Production Co. Nigeria.

The $35.1 million deal will boost the lithium development company's reserves and resources by 30%, according to a Monday release. The acquired company's main asset is a 49% non-operated interest in the Stubb Creek oil & gas field in Akwa Ibom State, Nigeria.

The agreement was disclosed in March 2024.

data.title: XPeng Raised to Buy From Neutral by Citigroup >XPEV
Stifel Adjusts Price Target on Amazon.com to $275 From $245, Maintains Buy Rating
data.description: Amazon.com has an average rating of buy and mean price target of $267.93, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: Zhejiang Leapmotor Technology Recorded Positive Net Profit for Single Quarter One Year Ahead of Target>9863.HK
Zhejiang Leapmotor Technology Achieved Positive Net Profit of CNY80M in 4Q24>9863.HK
Italian Stocks Edge Lower
data.description:
The FTSE MIB edged 0.2% lower to around 38,500 in early trading on Monday, as mounting concerns of a broader trade war and its impact on the U.S. economy kept investors on edge.

Additionally, China’s deflationary pressures further weighed on market sentiment.

Financial stocks led the decline, with UniCredit (-1.4%), Intesa Sanpaolo (-1.2%), FinecoBank (-2.6%), BPER Banca (-2.8%), Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena (-2.2%), and Banca Generali (-1.7%) trading in the red.

On the other hand, notable gains were recorded by Leonardo (+1.7%), Amplifon (+1.5%), Hera (+2%), and Enel (+1.1%).

data.title: 2025 Likely a Critical Year for Humanoid Robots Launches, Daiwa Says — Market Talk
data.description: 2025 could be a key year for humanoid robots launches, Daiwa analysts say in a research note. Some Chinese robot makers, like Unitree and AgiBot, have already reduced unit costs to around 100,000 yuan, with the potential for further reductions of more than 50% upon mass-production, they say. Daiwa expects more than one million humanoid robot units to be produced by 2030, while further AI breakthroughs could enhance robots' responsiveness and accelerate their adoption. They anticipate that humanoid robots will be initially used in manufacturing and logistics before expanding into the construction and mining industries. Several leading global tech companies, including Huawei and Meta, have already announced humanoid robot products. Daiwa expects these development to boost sentiment, establish an entirely new supply chain, and particularly benefit component suppliers. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)
